stories.headline: European M&A surges 23% to $818.9bn in 2025 as PE dry powder and private credit reshape UK deal structures heading into 2026
stories.summary: Announced M&A deal value for **EMEA** targets reached approximately **US$939.9 billion** in 2025, a **27.2% increase** from US$739.1 billion in 2024, according to Refinitiv data cited in the Lexology Global Private Equity Transactions overview. Europe alone accounted for **US$818.9 billion** of that total, up approximately 23% from US$665.9 billion in 2024. The UK market is exhibiting a 'quality over quantity' dynamic: **PwC** expects more deals to cross the finish line in 2026 as companies build investment portfolios across structurally attractive sectors, even as deal volumes remain broadly flat relative to value. **Private credit** has become the fastest-growing financing channel for large-cap transactions, with banks pulling back from corporate lending and private credit funds offering flexibility that syndicated loan markets cannot match — a structural shift noted explicitly by PwC's Stapleton. **Deloitte's 2026 M&A Trends Survey** found that 90% of PE respondents expect to increase both deal counts and aggregate value in 2026, driven partly by a backlog of deferred exits and mounting LP pressure on funds to return liquidity. As of October 2024, PE funds held approximately **£178 billion** in UK-focused dry powder, per Wedlake Bell analysis, creating significant deployment pressure. Sectors drawing the most attention include **technology and life sciences**, where government investment incentives are clearest, and businesses with resilient cash flows in a low-growth UK macro environment. Public-to-private transactions are a particular area of focus: UK mid-caps remain attractively valued relative to US peers, making them natural targets for large global PE funds. The **Middle East and Africa** segment of EMEA M&A also surged — up 65% year-on-year to US$120.9 billion across 1,835 deals — signalling that Gulf sovereign wealth and family office capital is increasingly active alongside traditional PE as an acquirer class in European processes. Shoosmiths notes the legal market must prepare to assist clients with exit readiness to ensure smoother and speedier processes, particularly given that sellers deferred exits through the more difficult second half of 2025. No single named adviser dominates the aggregate data, but the structural themes — private credit replacing bank debt, PE dominance of acquirer profiles, and a flight to quality assets — are the defining features of the 2026 pipeline. Specific deal conditions and break fees vary by transaction; the macro data does not report aggregate conditionality statistics.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Linklaters** and **Clifford Chance** are best positioned on large-cap European M&A given their pan-European coverage and longstanding relationships with the global PE houses most active in the EMEA market. **Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer** retains a dominant position in public-to-private transactions in the UK, where **Takeover Panel** process expertise and relationships with UK institutional shareholders are decisive. **Slaughter and May** is the natural choice for complex corporate governance work on UK-listed targets, particularly where independent board committee advice is required. The volume of **CMA merger control** filings expected from a busier 2026 pipeline will keep UK competition teams at all five Magic Circle firms fully occupied.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Kirkland & Ellis** is structurally dominant on UK and European PE buyout mandates, particularly for fund-level and co-investment structuring, and its leveraged finance team in London is well-placed to advise on private credit facilities replacing traditional bank debt. **Latham & Watkins** competes directly on leveraged finance and is increasingly winning mandates on large-cap European sponsor-to-sponsor secondaries and exits. The rise of **private credit** as the dominant financing channel means both firms' finance practices benefit disproportionately relative to Magic Circle rivals whose corporate finance heritage is more equity-oriented.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A first-seat trainee on a PE buyout at a Magic Circle or US firm would spend significant time maintaining **conditions precedent checklists**, tracking regulatory approval timelines across multiple jurisdictions, and coordinating with external competition counsel on **CMA Phase 1** filing preparation. On the financing side, trainees assist with due diligence on private credit facility terms, reviewing intercreditor arrangements and covenant packages. The 23% rise in European M&A value in 2025, against flat volumes, confirms the 'larger but fewer' deal cycle that has been reshaping associate staffing models at elite firms since 2023.

stories.talkingPoint: The most striking structural shift in European M&A right now is not deal volume but financing architecture: private credit has displaced syndicated bank debt as the dominant channel for large-cap transactions, and that single fact is reshaping which law firms — and which practice groups within them — capture the most valuable mandates. For Magic Circle firms built around equity capital markets, the PE buyout boom powered by private credit is a competitive threat as much as an opportunity, ceding leveraged finance ground to Kirkland and Latham. With 90% of PE respondents in Deloitte's survey expecting higher deal counts and values in 2026, the pipeline is real — but the legal fees will flow along the financing structure, not just the corporate layer.

stories.headline: Generate Biomedicines files for $400m US IPO led by Goodwin Procter and Latham & Watkins as biotech capital markets reopen
stories.summary: Asthma-focused biotech firm **Generate Biomedicines** filed plans with US regulators on 23 February 2026 to raise approximately **$400 million** in its initial public offering, with the mandate led by **Goodwin Procter LLP** and **Latham & Watkins LLP** as lead counsel, according to Law360. The filing signals a cautious reopening of the US biotech IPO window after a prolonged period of suppressed issuance in the sector. Generate Biomedicines is focused on **asthma therapeutics**, placing it in the high-value biologics segment of the biotech market, where investor appetite has historically been driven by pipeline differentiation and late-stage clinical data rather than revenue metrics. The deal is structured as a conventional IPO with SEC registration, meaning the company will be subject to the full **Securities Act of 1933** disclosure regime and ongoing **Exchange Act** reporting obligations post-listing. The $400 million target is an indicative range at the filing stage and subject to book-building; final pricing will depend on roadshow reception and prevailing market conditions at the time of pricing. No underwriter economics, lock-up terms, or use-of-proceeds breakdown were reported beyond the headline fundraise figure at the filing stage. The concurrent hiring of **Cleary Gottlieb** partners from **Allen & Overy Shearman** to reinforce its capital markets team — reported separately by Law360 — underscores that elite US firms are actively building capacity in anticipation of a busier IPO and debt issuance pipeline in 2026. In the UK context, the **Spring Statement** forecast of UK GDP growth slowing to **1.1% in 2026** before recovering creates a nuanced backdrop for London-listed issuers, making the US market relatively more attractive for high-growth biotech seeking deeper liquidity pools. No UK or European advisers were named in connection with the Generate Biomedicines filing. The deal remains subject to SEC review and market conditions; no pricing date has been publicly confirmed.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Herbert Smith Freehills** and **Ashurst** have built credible US Securities Act capability in London and would be natural choices for any European biotech considering a dual-track US/UK listing. **Linklaters** and **Freshfields** retain the deepest ECM benches in the London market for investment-grade and high-growth issuers, and both will be monitoring whether the Generate Biomedicines filing signals a durable reopening of the biotech IPO window that could spill over into European markets. The **FCA**'s PISCES framework and the reformed UK listing rules are specifically designed to compete for exactly this category of high-growth issuer.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Latham & Watkins** is the standout beneficiary here — its life sciences capital markets practice is among the most active globally, and leading a $400 million biotech IPO reinforces its position as the go-to firm for founder-led, VC-backed biotech issuers. **Goodwin Procter** is the dominant firm for emerging growth and biotech company-side mandates, with a market share in US biotech IPOs that rivals any Magic Circle equivalent in European ECM. **Cleary Gottlieb**'s lateral hire of an A&O Shearman capital markets duo signals that even top-tier US firms view 2026 as a year requiring additional bench depth.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee on a US-registered IPO at a London-based firm would spend the early weeks of a deal working on **verification** of the registration statement — cross-referencing every factual claim in the prospectus against primary source documents — and drafting **comfort letter** requests to auditors. They would also assist with maintaining the disclosure letter and tracking **SEC comment letters** during the review period. The biotech IPO cycle is tightly correlated with Fed rate policy: with markets pricing in fewer cuts than previously anticipated, the window for $400 million+ biotech issuances remains narrow.

stories.talkingPoint: Generate Biomedicines' $400 million IPO filing — with Latham and Goodwin on the ticket — is the clearest signal yet that US biotech capital markets are reopening after two years of near-dormancy, and the legal fees generated by a single large biotech IPO can rival those of a mid-market M&A transaction given the intensity of SEC disclosure work. For London-based trainees, the more interesting question is whether this signals a parallel reopening on this side of the Atlantic under the FCA's reformed listing rules, or whether US markets will continue to capture the high-growth biotech issuances that London has historically struggled to attract.

stories.headline: Hogan Lovells advises SES on $3.1bn acquisition of Intelsat in landmark satellite infrastructure consolidation deal
stories.summary: **Hogan Lovells** has advised **SES**, the Luxembourg-headquartered satellite operator, on its **US$3.1 billion acquisition of Intelsat**, a deal described by the firm as a 'transformational' transaction in the global satellite communications infrastructure sector. The deal creates one of the largest commercial satellite operators in the world, combining SES's multi-orbit fleet — which spans geostationary and medium earth orbit assets — with Intelsat's extensive geostationary satellite network serving government, aviation, maritime, and broadcast customers globally. The deal value of **US$3.1 billion** is as reported by Hogan Lovells on their transaction credentials page; no further breakdown of debt-versus-equity financing or specific lender group has been publicly reported in the available sources. The strategic rationale is consolidation-driven: the satellite industry has faced sustained margin pressure from low-earth orbit entrants including **SpaceX's Starlink** and **Amazon's Project Kuiper**, and scale is increasingly necessary to compete for large government and enterprise connectivity contracts. SES is listed on **Euronext Luxembourg and Paris**, adding a cross-border European securities law dimension to the transaction alongside the underlying asset deal. The transaction would require regulatory clearance from multiple jurisdictions given Intelsat's extensive US government contract book and the national security sensitivity of satellite communications infrastructure — likely involving review by the **Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (CFIUS)** and the **Federal Communications Commission (FCC)**. Hogan Lovells is specifically noted as having a dedicated international trade and investment practice, which would be directly relevant to navigating the CFIUS process for a European acquirer of a US-headquartered satellite operator. No other advisers — on the Intelsat side or on financing — are named in the available source material. The deal sits at the intersection of **energy infrastructure**, **national security**, and **telecommunications regulation**, making it one of the more complex regulatory approval processes in the current market.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Hogan Lovells** is the named adviser here and its dual US-UK structure gives it a decisive advantage on transactions requiring simultaneous **CFIUS**, **FCC**, and European regulatory navigation — the precise combination this deal demands. **Clifford Chance** and **Freshfields** have strong satellite and telecommunications regulatory practices in London and Brussels respectively, and would be natural competition for the European regulatory clearance workstreams. **Herbert Smith Freehills** has a growing infrastructure and energy disputes practice that would be relevant if any regulatory challenge materialises.
whyItMatters.usFirms: The **CFIUS** review of a European acquirer taking control of a satellite operator with deep US government contracts is precisely the type of national security-adjacent transaction where **Sullivan & Cromwell** and **Skadden Arps** compete most aggressively — both have dedicated CFIUS practices in Washington DC that regularly advise on FCC licensing and national security agreement negotiations. **Latham & Watkins** would be competitive on the financing side if a leveraged structure is confirmed. The **FY 2026 NDAA**'s explicit treatment of satellite and communications platforms as national security assets adds regulatory complexity that strengthens the case for US specialist counsel.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee on a cross-border infrastructure acquisition of this scale would be occupied with **jurisdictional mapping** — identifying every regulatory body requiring notification or approval across the US, EU, and other markets where the target holds licences — and maintaining a master regulatory timeline. On the CFIUS workstream specifically, trainees assist with drafting the **voluntary notice** and compiling the transaction documents, ownership charts, and government contract disclosures required for submission. The SES-Intelsat combination is a case study in how the **national security regulatory perimeter** around infrastructure assets has expanded dramatically since 2020, adding months and significant legal cost to deals that would previously have been treated as purely commercial transactions.

stories.talkingPoint: The SES acquisition of Intelsat for $3.1 billion is the defining satellite infrastructure consolidation of the current cycle — a direct response to Starlink's market disruption — and Hogan Lovells' role on the deal illustrates why international trade and investment practices are now core revenue generators at elite transactional firms, not niche add-ons. The CFIUS dimension alone on a European acquirer buying a US government satellite contractor will generate a legal workstream that rivals the core M&A advice in complexity and billing hours, and firms without credible Washington national security practices are increasingly locked out of this category of deal.

stories.headline: CMA issues fourth informal sustainability agreement approval while UK government consults on accountability reforms to the competition regime under January 2026 paper
stories.summary: The **UK Competition and Markets Authority** has issued its **fourth informal approval** under UK competition law of a sustainability agreement between competitors, according to the McGuireWoods European Competition Law Newsletter for March 2026. The decision continues the CMA's developing practice of providing comfort to businesses seeking to cooperate on environmental and sustainability objectives — an area where the boundary between permissible collaboration and **Chapter I Competition Act 1998** infringement has been heavily debated since the CMA published its sustainability guidance. The specific businesses involved in the fourth approval are not identified in the available source, but the approval confirms the CMA's appetite to facilitate legitimate sustainability cooperation rather than treat all competitor interaction as presumptively anticompetitive. Separately, the UK government published a significant consultation paper on **20 January 2026** titled 'Refining Our Competition Regime: Driving Growth and Enhancing Competition for Businesses and Consumers', which proposes reforms targeting **CMA accountability in merger and markets decisions**, enhancements to the CMA's markets work, and reform of market remedies. The consultation signals that the new CMA Chair **Doug Gurr** — confirmed earlier this year — will operate under a reformed statutory framework designed to better align competition enforcement with the government's growth agenda. The McGuireWoods newsletter also highlights a European Commission abuse of dominance case involving a **Unilateral Pricing and Investment Policy (UPIP)**, noting that the enforcement risk for non-obvious infringements is manageable where conduct can be modified after investigation begins — a practically important observation for compliance teams advising on novel commercial arrangements. The **Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 (DMCCA)** is simultaneously entering implementation phase, with new consumer protection and digital markets powers being activated progressively through 2026. The concurrent EU **Digital Fairness Act** is at advanced planning stage and described in available sources as potentially the most significant EU consumer reform since the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive. UK firms advising on competition compliance face the challenge of tracking two diverging but overlapping regulatory regimes simultaneously following Brexit.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Linklaters** and **Freshfields** have the deepest UK and EU competition law benches among Magic Circle firms and are best placed to advise on both the CMA's sustainability guidance practice and the government's consultation on accountability reforms. **Clifford Chance**'s competition group is particularly strong on **Chapter I** cartel and horizontal agreement work, making it the natural choice for clients seeking informal comfort or navigating the sustainability cooperation approval process. **Herbert Smith Freehills** has invested heavily in its competition practice and regularly acts in **CMA Phase 2** investigations and appeals before the **Competition Appeal Tribunal**, giving it strong standing on the market remedies reform workstream.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Cleary Gottlieb** is the leading US firm for EU competition law in Brussels and London, and the parallel evolution of **European Commission** abuse of dominance enforcement — illustrated by the UPIP case — keeps it central to multinational compliance mandates. **Skadden Arps** competes strongly on US-origin merger control filings with a UK nexus, and the CMA accountability reforms may materially alter the Phase 2 investigation timeline that Skadden's London competition team must plan around.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee in a competition team would currently be tracking the government's **January 2026 consultation** response deadline and summarising submissions from industry bodies for partner review — a core document-management and research task. On sustainability agreements, trainees assist with drafting the **pre-notification submissions** to the CMA and preparing the commercial rationale documents that accompany requests for informal guidance. The fourth CMA sustainability approval in this series is a data point confirming that environmental cooperation frameworks are becoming a standard compliance product across sectors ranging from packaging to energy, with legal spend growing accordingly.

stories.talkingPoint: The CMA's fourth sustainability agreement approval is a quiet but commercially significant development: it confirms that the regulator is willing to provide meaningful comfort to businesses cooperating on environmental goals, and that companies which engage proactively with the CMA's guidance process can manage their competition law exposure even in legally novel territory. For lawyers, the January 2026 government consultation on CMA accountability reform is potentially more consequential — if enacted, proposals to enhance accountability in merger and markets decisions could fundamentally alter the appeal dynamics that currently make Phase 2 investigations so commercially disruptive for clients.

stories.headline: Pan-NOx Dieselgate litigation involving over 1.6 million claimants against Mercedes-Benz, Ford, Nissan, and Stellantis reaches critical liability and quantum trial phase in 2026
stories.summary: The **Pan-NOx litigation** — formally **Various Claimants v. Mercedes-Benz Group AG, Ford Motor Company, Nissan Motor Co Ltd, Stellantis NV and others** in the High Court (Commercial Court) — is one of the largest consumer group actions in English legal history, involving **over 1.6 million claimants** alleging that vehicle manufacturers used unlawful 'defeat devices' to manipulate emissions tests, according to Morrison Foerster's UK Litigation 2025 Recap and 2026 Outlook. Major **liability and quantum trials** are listed through 2026, making this a defining year for the litigation. The proceedings have already prompted detailed **judicial scrutiny of costs and case management**, resulting in reductions of more than half in costs budgets on some elements of the claim — a signal that courts are actively managing the economics of mega group actions rather than allowing uncapped litigation spending. The case sits alongside **Município de Mariana and others v. BHP Group (UK) Ltd**, another multi-defendant environmental group action in the Technology and Construction Court, as part of a wider wave of **environmental and consumer group litigation** before the English courts. Early 2026 data from **Solomonic** shows that **12,301 claims** were issued in UK courts in 2025, compared to **11,165 in 2024** — a 10% increase confirming the litigation market's growth trajectory. The **Pan-NOx** defendants face claims that go to the core of **product liability**, **consumer protection**, and **tort law** in an international context: the manufacturers are headquartered in Germany, the US, Japan, and the Netherlands respectively, giving the litigation a significant cross-border enforcement dimension. No specific settlement figures, interim awards, or specific counsel are named in the available source material. The Morrison Foerster analysis notes that the Commercial Court's attraction of 'mega' cases — including SKAT and PIFSS in 2024 and 2025 — is producing judicial decisions of unprecedented length and complexity, a trend continuing into 2026.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Herbert Smith Freehills** and **Freshfields** have dominant positions in defending major group actions and mass tort litigation for FTSE 100 and international corporate defendants, and one or more of them is likely to appear on the defence side of the Pan-NOx litigation. **Linklaters** has a strong Commercial Court litigation practice and experience in multi-party international disputes. **Slaughter and May** would be a natural choice for any UK-domiciled defendant requiring advice on the intersection of regulatory and litigation exposure. The scale of costs budgeting work — and the judicial reductions already applied — means that **costs lawyers and litigation funders** are playing an unusually prominent role alongside traditional solicitors.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Cleary Gottlieb** and **Sullivan & Cromwell** are the strongest US firms for defending European manufacturers in English court group actions, given their ability to coordinate US, EU, and UK litigation strategies simultaneously across a global manufacturer defendant. **Kirkland & Ellis** has invested significantly in its London disputes practice and competes for large-scale commercial litigation mandates. The multi-jurisdictional nature of the defendants — German, American, Japanese, Dutch — means US firms with European platform capability have a genuine competitive advantage over purely domestic UK dispute resolution practices.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee on a mass group action of this scale would spend significant time on **disclosure review** — reviewing and categorising documents in a managed review platform — and assisting with the preparation of **witness statements** and **expert briefs** ahead of the liability trials. Maintaining the claims database for 1.6 million claimants requires close coordination between legal teams and litigation support specialists, giving trainees early exposure to the technology infrastructure of modern mega-litigation. The 10% year-on-year increase in UK claims issuance from Solomonic's data suggests that demand for litigation trainees and junior associates is structurally increasing, not cyclically.

stories.talkingPoint: The Pan-NOx Dieselgate litigation — 1.6 million claimants, four major global manufacturers, liability and quantum trials running through 2026 — is the clearest evidence that English courts have become the global venue of choice for mass cross-border consumer group actions, and that is generating sustained high-value work for the litigation practices of every elite firm in London. What makes it commercially distinctive for law firms is the cost management pressure: courts are actively cutting costs budgets by more than half, which means that only firms with highly efficient litigation infrastructure and strong project management capability can profitably run these matters.

stories.headline: Hogan Lovells advises Ukraine on landmark mineral rights agreement with the United States as UK-EU TCA review opens in 2026
stories.summary: **Hogan Lovells** has advised **Ukraine** on a landmark **mineral rights agreement with the United States**, described on the firm's international trade and investment page as a significant transaction in the firm's recent credentials. The agreement — the precise terms of which are not detailed in the available source — reflects the growing use of **natural resource agreements** as instruments of geopolitical alignment and reconstruction financing, with the US securing preferential access to Ukrainian critical minerals in exchange for continued support. The transaction sits at the intersection of **public international law**, **extractive industry regulation**, and **bilateral investment treaty** frameworks, and represents exactly the type of sovereign advisory mandate that major international law firms are competing aggressively to win in the current geopolitical environment. Separately, the **2026 review of the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement (TCA)** has formally opened, with the review scope covering trade in services and investment under **Article 126** of the TCA and the broader review mechanism under **Article 776**. The Parliamentary Research Briefing confirms that the TCA review does **not extend to financial services** — a point of significant practical importance for London's financial sector, which remains outside the TCA framework and dependent on **equivalence decisions** and third-country access regimes. The review includes the possibility of improvements to provisions on trade in services and investment, including a review of all non-conforming measures and reservations set out in the TCA's annexes. The **Common Understanding** between the UK and EU on SPS and ETS linkage — also under discussion — involves **dynamic alignment** obligations requiring the UK to implement relevant EU rules, raising constitutional questions about parliamentary sovereignty. No specific timeline for completion of the TCA review is stated in the available source.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Hogan Lovells** has demonstrated its sovereign advisory capability with the Ukraine mandate and is specifically noted as having been appointed to the **UK Government Trade Negotiations Panel** — giving it a structural advantage in UK government-facing trade law mandates including the TCA review process. **Linklaters** and **Freshfields** advise major UK financial institutions on the implications of the TCA's exclusion of financial services, and both will be active in submissions to the TCA review from the financial sector. **Clifford Chance** has one of the strongest international trade and EU regulatory practices in London and is well-placed on the ETS linkage dynamic alignment workstream.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Cleary Gottlieb** and **Sullivan & Cromwell** have deep sovereign advisory practices and are natural competitors to Hogan Lovells for the Ukraine mineral rights mandate and comparable **bilateral investment** agreements. US firms with Washington DC public international law benches — including **Skadden** — are increasingly active in European sovereign advisory work given the US government's growing role as a financial and legal counterparty in European reconstruction and energy security transactions.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee on the TCA review workstream would assist with **treaty text analysis** — mapping the existing non-conforming measures in the annexes against proposed reforms and identifying where UK-specific reservations create commercial constraints for clients in services sectors. On sovereign advisory matters like the Ukraine minerals deal, trainees draft sections of the **due diligence memoranda** on applicable international law frameworks including **ICSID convention** jurisdiction and **bilateral investment treaty** protections. The TCA's explicit exclusion of financial services from the 2026 review is a concrete data point that UK financial sector access to EU markets will remain governed by unilateral equivalence decisions — a structural commercial risk that generates sustained legal advisory demand.

stories.talkingPoint: Hogan Lovells advising Ukraine on a mineral rights agreement with the US is a signal that the most commercially significant international mandates right now are not traditional cross-border M&A but sovereign and quasi-sovereign transactions at the intersection of geopolitics and natural resources law — and that firms positioned on government trade panels and with credible public international law capacity are winning them. The TCA review's explicit exclusion of financial services is equally significant: it confirms that London's financial sector will continue to operate outside any preferential trade framework with the EU indefinitely, sustaining demand for regulatory access advice that is unlikely to disappear regardless of how UK-EU relations evolve.

stories.headline: Close to 80% of US lawyers now use AI in practice as law firms face mounting compliance obligations under new 2026 state AI laws and EU AI Act implementation
stories.summary: According to **Clio's Legal Trends Report** cited in JD Supra's AI Legal Compliance analysis, close to **80% of lawyers in the United States** now report using AI in their practice — an increase of nearly **60 percentage points** compared to just two years ago. The scale of adoption creates significant compliance and professional responsibility obligations for law firms, as AI-generated outputs carry risks of **hallucination**, confidentiality breach, and inadvertent waiver of privilege that existing professional conduct frameworks did not anticipate. From **1 January 2026**, three California statutes have simultaneously come into force: the **California Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act (TFAIA/SB 53)**, which imposes oversight and reporting requirements on frontier AI developers; the **California Generative AI: Training Data Transparency Act (AB 2013)**; and the **California AI Content Transparency Act (SB 942)**, which requires disclosure obligations for AI-generated content. The **Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act (HB 149)** also became effective on 1 January 2026. The **Colorado AI Act (SB 24-205)** enters force on **30 June 2026**, imposing operational deadlines on high-risk AI systems. At the federal level, the White House issued an **Executive Order** on 11 December 2025 titled 'Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence', which signals an intent to create a federal framework that may preempt state laws — though the EO itself does not create binding preemption. For law firms specifically, the JD Supra analysis notes that clients increasingly expect their legal representatives to use AI safely and effectively, and that failure to do so creates both professional and reputational risk. The **EU AI Act** implementation timeline continues to run in parallel, with high-risk AI system obligations applying progressively from August 2026. UK firms advising multinational clients must track the interaction between **EU AI Act** obligations, US state laws, and the UK's currently sector-led approach to AI regulation.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Allen & Overy Shearman** — now rebranded after its combination — has been among the most public about AI adoption through its **Harvey** deployment, which has become a benchmark against which other Magic Circle firms measure their own AI investment strategies. **Clifford Chance** and **Linklaters** have both announced significant AI initiatives targeting document review, contract analysis, and legal research automation. The compliance obligations imposed by the **EU AI Act** on high-risk AI systems will generate substantial advisory mandates for TMT and regulatory practices at all major UK firms advising technology sector clients.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Latham & Watkins** and **Kirkland & Ellis** have both invested heavily in AI tooling for their transactional practices, with document comparison and due diligence automation now standard in large PE deal processes. **Davis Polk** has been noted for its structured approach to AI governance for financial institution clients — directly relevant as banks and asset managers navigate the compliance obligations created by the new California and Texas AI statutes. The proliferation of US state-level AI laws creates a **multi-state compliance** advisory practice area that plays to the strength of large US firms with broad domestic geographic coverage.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee or junior associate working on an AI compliance matter in 2026 would be engaged in **regulatory mapping** — tracking the effective dates, scope definitions, and territorial reach of the California TFAIA, AB 2013, SB 942, Texas HB 149, and Colorado SB 24-205 simultaneously — and preparing client alerts summarising each statute's obligations for technology company clients. On the internal firm side, trainees increasingly interact with AI-assisted **due diligence platforms** and **contract review tools** as standard workflow components, requiring them to understand both the efficiency gains and the verification obligations that come with AI-assisted output. The 60-percentage-point increase in US lawyer AI adoption in two years is the fastest tool adoption curve in the legal profession's recorded history, and the compliance infrastructure to manage it is still being built.

stories.talkingPoint: The fact that 80% of US lawyers are already using AI — up nearly 60 points in two years — means that AI adoption in legal practice has crossed from early-adopter novelty to professional baseline expectation faster than any previous legal technology wave, including the shift to electronic disclosure. The more interesting regulatory story for commercial lawyers is not the EU AI Act in isolation but the collision between five or more concurrent US state AI statutes, a federal preemption signal, and EU obligations — a multi-jurisdictional compliance problem that will generate significant advisory fees for the TMT and regulatory practices of every firm with a US-European client base.

sectorWatch.trend: Private Credit Displacing Syndicated Bank Debt
sectorWatch.body: Private credit has become the fastest-growing financing channel for large-cap European M&A, with banks pulling back from corporate lending and PE sponsors exploiting the flexibility that direct lending funds offer relative to syndicated markets. **PwC** and **Deloitte's 2026 M&A Trends Survey** both identify this structural shift as the defining feature of 2026 deal financing, with 90% of PE respondents expecting higher deal activity. For law firms, the shift concentrates leveraged finance mandates at **Kirkland & Ellis** and **Latham & Watkins** in London, where private credit fund relationships are strongest, at the expense of Magic Circle rivals whose corporate banking relationships were built around the now-retreating syndicated loan market. With approximately **£178 billion** in UK-focused PE dry powder awaiting deployment, the volume of private credit-financed transactions in 2026 is set to be substantial.

oneToFollow.story: The UK government's January 2026 consultation 'Refining Our Competition Regime' — proposing enhanced CMA accountability in merger and markets decisions — closes for responses and moves toward potential legislative implementation
oneToFollow.why: If enacted, proposals to reform CMA accountability mechanisms in Phase 2 investigations and market remedies could fundamentally alter the timeline and predictability of UK merger clearance, affecting every large M&A transaction with a UK nexus and generating a sustained advisory workstream for competition practices at Magic Circle and Silver Circle firms. Corporate and competition lawyers at firms including **Freshfields**, **Linklaters**, and **Herbert Smith Freehills** will need to track the consultation outcome closely, as any statutory change to CMA decision-making standards will directly reshape the risk allocation in deal signing and conditions precedent drafting.
